reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary of the Paper:  
This paper presents a practical framework for learning complex locomotion skills in soft, deformable agents by leveraging fully differentiable physics simulators. Building on recent work such as ChainQueen (Hu et al., 2019) and DiffTaichi (Hu et al., 2020), the authors systematically incorporate a suite of design improvements—including periodic sine‐based activation functions inspired by SIREN, task‐specific loss functions that account for periodicity and delayed evaluation, a modified Adam optimizer with reduced momentum, and batched simulator instances—to train a single neural network to perform multiple locomotion skills (running with controllable velocity, jumping with controllable height, turning, and crawling) across both mass‐spring systems and MLS‐MPM backends. The manuscript includes extensive ablation studies (Table 1, Fig. 5) that quantify the impact of each component, and benchmark comparisons against PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), demonstrating faster convergence and more stable training in multi‐goal settings.

The paper walks through the differentiable simulation environments (Section 2), describes the agent structures and actuation models (Fig. 2, 3), and details the architecture and training procedure of the NN controller (two-layer FC network with sine activations, periodic control signals, state‐centralized inputs, and duplicated goal channels). In experiments (Section 4) the authors validate the stability of end‐to‐end gradients over hundreds of time steps (Fig. 6), tune Adam’s β₁, β₂ (Table 2), and demonstrate that without techniques such as batching or the tailored loss design, training either fails to converge or oscillates. The framework is also shown to work interactively, allowing user‐driven changes to velocity and height targets in real time.

Strengths:  
1) Comprehensive Ablation Analysis and Practical Guidance.  
   The paper’s most significant asset is its detailed investigation of each design choice. For instance, the ablation study in Table 1 shows that removing periodic signals (“Full-PS”) or sine activations (“Full-AF”) degrades normalized validation task loss from ~0.58 to ~0.84 and ~0.98 respectively on the Alpaca agent. Figure 5 further visualizes how the “Full” method converges an order of magnitude faster than variants missing a key component. These empirical insights into optimizer hyperparameters, activation functions, batching, and loss components will be highly useful to practitioners looking to apply differentiable physics to other domains.  
2) Multi‐Skill, Multi‐Backend Demonstration.  
   Unlike prior work that typically focuses on a single locomotion direction or rigid‐body tasks, this framework successfully trains soft agents with tens or hundreds of degrees of freedom to both run and jump at user‐specified speeds and heights, and to rotate in 3D (Section 3.1, Loss in 3D). The fact that a single NN controller can interpolate smoothly across goals—switching from low‐velocity jumps to high‐speed runs without retraining—shows the potential for more complex, continuous control tasks.  
3) Clear Empirical Superiority over PPO.  
   Figure 9 compares differentiable physics training to PPO in multi‐goal settings and illustrates that PPO often stalls or overfits to a single skill, whereas the proposed method steadily reduces task, running, and jumping losses. This direct comparison underscores the benefit of gradient‐based approaches when accurate simulator gradients are available.

Weaknesses:  
1) Technical Novelty and Simulator Contribution Are Lean.  
   The manuscript relies on existing differentiable simulation backends (DiffTaichi and MLS‐MPM) with relatively minor adapations, such as using dashpot damping in the mass‐spring model and dynamic background grids for MPM (Appendix C). The claim of “end‐to‐end differentiable simulation environment” is not supported by substantial new simulation algorithms; Section 2 largely summarizes standard material point and spring‐mass models. A more detailed breakdown of what was engineered beyond plugging in DiffTaichi would strengthen the second claimed contribution.  
2) Limited Task Diversity beyond Flat‐Ground Locomotion.  
   While running, jumping, and turning on flat surfaces are incrementally richer than single‐direction locomotion, the paper stops short of evaluating the framework on uneven terrains, obstacle courses, or purely manipulation tasks (e.g., object pushing or grasping). Although differentiable simulation could in principle handle these, the current experiments do not demonstrate such versatility, leaving open whether the observed design principles (sine activations, periodic control signals) carry over to other domains like manipulation or navigation in cluttered environments.

Additional Questions and Comments:  
1) Dependency on Simulator Differentiability.  
   Section 2.1 states that “any physically‐based deformable object simulator can be applied,” yet the framework requires accurate analytic gradients. Could the authors clarify whether approximate differentiable simulators (e.g., learned physics proxies) or black‐box simulators with finite‐difference gradients would work, or is the framework strictly limited to adjoint‐based implementations?  
2) Generalization of Ablation Findings.  
   The importance of SIREN‐style sine activations and periodic inputs is well supported for cyclical locomotion, but how critical are these for non‐periodic or contact‐rich tasks? An empirical or theoretical discussion on when to apply these design choices would be helpful.  
3) Hyperparameter Sensitivity.  
   Table 2 shows a grid search over Adam’s β parameters for the Alpaca agent. How consistent are these optimal β₁, β₂ values across different agent geometries (e.g., Monster, HugeStool)? Is there a recommended default or should practitioners always perform a grid search?  
4) Ablation on Regularization Loss.  
   The manuscript introduces an actuation regularizer (Eq. 5) but does not show an ablation explicitly removing it. Data on how training behaves without this term would complete the analysis in Section 3.2.

Conclusion and Recommendation:  
Overall, this work provides a valuable empirical study of how to combine differentiable physics with careful network and loss design to achieve multi‐skill soft agent locomotion. Although its technical novelty in the simulator itself is limited, the depth of the ablation studies and practical insights into activation functions, optimizer settings, batching, and loss formulation make it a useful reference for researchers and engineers aiming to deploy gradient‐based control approaches. I therefore believe the paper merits acceptance in ICLR and recommend a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Complex Locomotion Skill Learning via Differentiable Physics”

Summary of the Paper  
This paper presents a framework to train neural network (NN) controllers for soft-body locomotion tasks by leveraging end-to-end differentiable physical simulators. The authors integrate several practical enhancements—including periodic (sine) activations inspired by SIREN, tailored multi-goal loss functions for running, jumping, and crawling, and large-scale batching with a modified Adam optimizer—to improve convergence speed and robustness. Experiments span mass-spring and MLS-MPM simulation backends, a range of 2D and 3D soft-robot geometries (Fig. 2–3), and multiple locomotion goals that can be switched interactively (Sec. 5, Fig. 11). Comparisons against PPO demonstrate up to an order-of-magnitude faster convergence on combined velocity and height tracking tasks (Sec 4.5, Fig. 9).

Strengths  
* +++ Interactive Control and Versatility: The system supports real-time user steering of learned soft robots. As shown in Fig. 11 and the supplemental video, the same NN controller smoothly interpolates between running at different velocities, jumping to specified heights, and even rotating in 3D. This level of live manipulation—across two simulator backends and multiple agent morphologies—is novel in differentiable-physics work and is an impressive demonstration of robustness.  
* +++ Convergence Efficiency: In Section 4.2 (Fig. 6), the authors provide a gradient-norm analysis over thousands of iterations, showing stable, non-exploding gradients when unrolling the differentiable simulator for hundreds of steps. Combined with GPU-parallelized batching (Sec 4.3, right of Fig. 5), training converges in a few thousand iterations versus tens of thousands for PPO (Sec 4.5, Fig. 9).  
* +++ Detailed Ablations: The paper includes an extensive ablation suite (Sec 4.3–4.4, Table 1, Tables 4–6 in appendix) that isolates the impact of optimizer choice, activation function, loss design, batch size, periodic signal input, state-vector features, and target channels. This empirical rigor—across four agent designs (“Alpaca”, “Monster”, “HugeStool”, “Stool”)—provides strong evidence of which components drive performance in this domain.

Weaknesses  
--- 1. Limited Technical Novelty of Loss Design  
  While the paper introduces tailored loss functions for velocity tracking (Eq. 4), height-based jumping (Eq. 2), and crawling (Eq. 3), all are specialized to periodic locomotion tasks. It remains unclear how these loss terms would adapt to non-locomotion behaviors—e.g., manipulation (pushing, grasping) or contact-rich tasks in the real world. The authors mention in Sec. 6 (“Limitations”) that robot design and physical parameters heavily influence task difficulty, but do not test loss generalization beyond simple benchmost tasks like juggling (Appendix F). A broader investigation—perhaps on rigid-body locomotion or manipulation controllers—would strengthen claims of a generally applicable loss framework.  

--- 2. Insufficient Justification for Sine Activations  
  The claim that periodic activations “significantly improve training” (Sec 3.3) is not fully supported by the experimental data. In Table 1 (agent Alpaca), sine yields only marginal gains over tanh (Task loss 0.82 vs. 0.84). Conversely, Table 5 in the appendix (agent HugeStool) shows tanh outperforming sine on Task loss. These conflicting results raise questions about consistency across morphologies. Moreover, common activations such as ReLU or GELU are only mentioned in passing in Fig. 7, without a clear quantitative summary. A more systematic comparison—ideally with detailed statistics on all four agents—would be necessary to convincingly isolate the benefit of sine over alternative nonlinearities.  

--- 3. Presentation and Clarity Issues  
  1. **Modified Adam**: The paper credits a “modified Adam optimizer” with improved momentum hyperparameters (Sec 4.2, Table 2), but does not clearly state the precise changes—e.g., reducing β₂ from 0.999 to 0.9—and compare to vanilla Adam in a standalone table. A succinct summary of the new default hyperparameters and their direct impact (beyond Fig. 5, left) would help reproducibility.  
  2. **Robot Names**: Figures and tables refer to agent names such as “Alpaca” and “HugeStool” (Fig. 2, Table 1), but these monikers are only introduced later in Sec. 4.5. Readers struggle to match the whimsical labels to specific geometries in Fig. 2–3. An early naming convention—perhaps a brief table mapping each name to its shape—would clarify the narrative.  
  3. **Incorrect Figure Reference**: In Sec. 4.2, the text refers to “Fig. 5” to illustrate SGD vs. Adam performance. However, Fig. 5 collates ablations of different components rather than optimizer comparison (that is instead in Fig. 6). This inconsistency interrupts the reader’s flow and should be corrected to the proper subplot.  

Despite these points, the paper presents a well-engineered system, comprehensive experiments, and compelling interactive demonstrations. However, the core novelty—while solidly executed—remains incremental, focused on practical tweaks rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theory.

Recommendation  
I recommend a rating of **5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**. The work offers strong empirical results and a valuable open-source differentiable physics controller pipeline, but the technical contributions (loss design scope, activation justification, and presentation clarity) require strengthening before acceptance.

reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice.

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.

Main Review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?

reviews.text:
Expanded Review of “On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features”

Summary of the Paper:  
This paper addresses the practical challenge of training Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) when node feature vectors are only partially observed. The authors propose a two‐stage framework in which missing features are first imputed via a diffusion process that minimizes the graph Dirichlet energy, and then a downstream GNN is applied for tasks such as node classification. By discretizing the gradient flow (heat equation) associated with the Dirichlet energy, they derive a simple iterative algorithm—Feature Propagation (FP)—which alternates a sparse matrix multiplication with the diffusion kernel and a resetting of known features. Empirically, FP + GNN outperforms a variety of baselines (including Zero/Random imputation, Neighbor Mean, MGCNN, GCNMF, PaGNN, and Label Propagation) across seven common benchmarks (Cora, CiteSeer, PubMed, Amazon‐Computers, Amazon‐Photo, OGBN‐Arxiv, and OGBN‐Products), and exhibits only a ∼4% relative accuracy drop even when 99% of features are missing. FP is also extremely fast—requiring only about 10 seconds on OGBN‐Products (∼2.5M nodes, 123M edges) on a single GPU—while some prior methods run out of memory at this scale.

Strengths:  
1. Clarity and Readability  
   - The paper is very well written and structured. Key derivations (from Dirichlet energy minimization to the diffusion PDE, and from there to the explicit Euler scheme) are presented clearly in Sections 3.1–3.2 and concretely illustrated in Algorithm 1.  
   - The accompanying Figure 2, which shows the low‐pass filtering effect of FP in the graph Fourier domain on Cora for varying rates of missing features, adds intuition about why FP produces smooth reconstructions.  

2. Theoretical Motivation and Elegance  
   - Deriving FP from the gradient flow of the Dirichlet energy provides a strong theoretical grounding. Proposition 1 and 2 (in the Appendix) ensure that the continuous PDE has a unique harmonic minimizer and that its discretization converges to the same solution.  
   - The interpretation of FP as an iterative low‐pass filter with known‐feature boundary conditions neatly bridges classical graph signal processing, PDEs on graphs, and modern GNN practice.  

3. Strong Empirical Performance  
   - On six common academic benchmarks plus the very large OGBN‐Products dataset, FP consistently outperforms all baselines. Table 1 shows that FP loses only 2.5% relative accuracy at 90% missing and 4.1% at 99% missing, whereas GCNMF and PaGNN suffer 53.3% and 21.3% drops, respectively.  
   - The authors also demonstrate that FP + GNN outperforms Label Propagation (LP) even at 99% missing features (Table 2), confirming that reconstructing continuous features remains beneficial compared to directly propagating discrete labels.  
   - Beyond accuracy, FP’s runtime is highly competitive. Figure 4 reports that FP is roughly 3× faster than PaGNN and GCNMF on Computers and OGBN‐Arxiv, and the 10s reconstruction time on the 2.5M‐node graph highlights its scalability advantage over OOM‐prone baselines.  

4. Thorough Experimental Analysis  
   - The paper investigates performance across a wide range of missing‐feature rates (0–99%) in Figure 3, revealing that all methods handle up to ∼50% missing reasonably well, but FP uniquely maintains high accuracy beyond 60% missing.  
   - The synthetic experiments on varying homophily levels (Figure 5) correctly show FP’s strengths under homophily and its limitations under low‐homophily settings, suggesting future directions for learnable, nonhomogeneous diffusion kernels.  
   - Ablations of downstream GNN architectures (GCN vs. GraphSAGE) and consistent hyperparameter settings reinforce the robustness of the empirical findings.  

Weaknesses and Questions:  
1. Extension to Vector-Valued Features  
   - The manuscript states (Section 3.3) that FP “extends to vector‐valued features by replacing x with X ∈ Rⁿˣᵈ, diffusing each channel independently.” It would be helpful to clarify whether the d channels are treated completely independently, or if one could account for channel correlations (e.g., via a learnable channel‐mixing diffusion operator). How would such an extension affect computational cost and convergence?  

2. Connection to Label Propagation (LP)  
   - While Section 3.4 contrasts FP with LP—highlighting that LP propagates discrete labels with a different diffusion operator—it remains somewhat unclear why the continuum diffusion of FP cannot be viewed simply as LP with a weighted or continuous update rule. From an algorithmic perspective, both involve iterations of the form x ← αŴx + (1−α)y. Could the authors provide a concrete comparison of the diffusion matrices and boundary‐condition updates to better differentiate FP from standard LP?  

3. Task-Agnostic vs. Task-Aware Imputation  
   - In the final paragraph of the Introduction, the paper suggests that task‐agnostic imputation (i.e., pre‐processing) might be less ideal than joint end‐to‐end learning. However, the experiments freeze the reconstructed features. Could the authors comment on whether jointly fine‐tuning the diffusion step along with the GNN (akin to SAT) might yield further gains, especially on moderate missing‐feature rates? Is it ever detrimental that FP is “unaware” of downstream labels?  

4. Figure 3 Readability and Baseline Behavior  
   - The axes labels and legend text in Figure 3 are fairly small and difficult to read. Enlarging fonts or using a higher‐resolution plot would improve clarity.  
   - The Random baseline is consistently far worse than Zero, which is somewhat counter‐intuitive: setting missing entries to zero might appear as a stronger perturbation than sampling from N(0,1). The paper briefly mentions (Sec. 5, Appendix A.4) that GNNs can learn to ignore zero features but not random noise—adding a succinct explanation in the main text would aid readers’ intuition.  

Minor Comments and Suggestions:  
– P1 (last paragraph): The statement that “being unaware of the task is arguably a bad thing” could be softened—task‐agnostic imputation may also have the benefit of generality, allowing the same diffusion to be reused across multiple tasks.  
– Appendix A.3 mentions a 50/40/10 split for training, validation, and test during MGCNN’s matrix‐completion pretraining. For reproducibility, please confirm that these proportions were fixed across datasets.  
– Table 3 (dataset statistics) lists OGBN‐Products features as size 100. It may be useful to note that these are textual embeddings from product descriptions, which motivates the high missing‐rate scenario when only some products have extended attributes.  

Recommendation: 8: Accept, good paper  
This work provides a conceptually simple yet theoretically motivated method for imputing missing graph features at scale. By leveraging Dirichlet energy minimization and a transparent diffusion‐based algorithm, it achieves state‐of‐the‐art performance in extremely high missing‐feature regimes while maintaining practical efficiency on million‐node graphs. Given the thorough experiments, clear writing, and the broad applicability to GNN architectures, I strongly support acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.
reviews.main_review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).

Summary Of The Review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.
reviews.main_review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.
reviews.main_review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.

Main Review:

2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

(Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. [1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

reviews.text:
Review for “On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features”

Summary of the Paper:
The manuscript proposes Feature Propagation (FP), a simple preprocessing algorithm to reconstruct missing node features in graphs by minimizing the Dirichlet energy. By interpreting the gradient flow of this energy as a diffusion equation on the graph and discretizing it via an explicit Euler scheme, the authors derive a fixed-point iteration (Equation 2) that alternates between a sparse matrix multiplication with the normalized adjacency matrix and resetting known features to their original values (Algorithm 1). In experiments on six standard node-classification benchmarks and the large-scale OGBN-Products dataset, FP combined with a downstream GNN (e.g., GCN or GraphSAGE) outperforms prior methods such as GCNMF and PaGNN—especially under extreme missing rates (up to 99% of features missing)—while remaining highly scalable (completing diffusion on ∼2.5 M nodes and ∼123 M edges in ∼10 s on one GPU). The authors also analyze runtime, show that FP is about 3× faster than GCNMF and PaGNN, and empirically demonstrate that FP’s effectiveness depends on graph homophily.

Strengths:
1. Clarity and Presentation: The paper is very clearly written and well organized. The derivation from Dirichlet energy minimization to the diffusion PDE and then to the iterative update is laid out in a self-contained manner. Figures such as the diagram in Figure 1 and the spectral analysis in Figure 2 aptly illustrate the core ideas.
2. Empirical Robustness at High Missing Rates: The most striking experimental finding is that FP suffers only a ~4% relative accuracy drop on average when as much as 99% of the feature entries are missing (Table 1), whereas GCNMF and PaGNN lose ~53% and ~21% accuracy, respectively. This result clearly demonstrates FP’s robustness to extreme sparsity, a regime not previously studied in detail.
3. Speed and Scalability: By reducing feature reconstruction to repeated sparse matrix–dense matrix products, FP operates in essentially linear time in the number of edges and requires only a few dozen iterations to converge. The reported runtimes (e.g., 10 s on OGBN-Products) highlight that FP can serve as a practical preprocessing step even for graphs with hundreds of millions of edges, whereas competing approaches run out of memory.

Weaknesses:
1. Novelty over Label Propagation (LP): While the authors note that FP handles continuous node features and LP propagates discrete labels, this distinction is largely terminological. Standard LP (Zhu & Ghahramani, 2002) already updates soft label distributions in a continuous space via an identical iterative scheme y ← α Ã y + (1–α) y₀. In FP, the update X ← Ã X followed by resetting X_k to the known features X_k is algorithmically equivalent to one variant of LP with boundary conditions. Moreover, interpreting LP through Dirichlet energy minimization has been discussed in prior work (e.g., Solomon et al. 2014 on Wasserstein propagation). The derivation in Section 3 thus appears to repackage well-known results under the guise of feature interpolation, without introducing a fundamentally new algorithmic principle.
2. Lack of Comparison to Topology‐Only Methods: The experimental evaluation focuses on methods that use at least some features, even if imputed (Random, Zero, Global Mean, Neighbor Mean, MGCNN, GCNMF, PaGNN). However, under high missingness regimes (e.g., ≥90%), graph topology alone can yield strong predictive performance. Classic approaches such as:
   • Label Propagation on node classes (directly propagating labels rather than reconstructing features),
   • Unsupervised embedding methods like DeepWalk, node2vec, or LINE followed by a downstream classifier,
often capture long-range dependencies and homophily in a manner very similar to FP. Without reporting results from these methods, it is unclear whether FP+i.e., GNN) really outperforms purely topology-based pipelines in the high-missingness setting, or simply matches their performance. In other words, FP may be no better than running LP on the labels directly or doing random-walk embeddings, which would call into question the need for a feature imputation step at all when features are almost entirely absent.
3. Limited Discussion of Heterophily: While Section 5 includes experiments on synthetic data with varying homophily (Figure 5), the paper stops short of proposing concrete extensions for low-homophily graphs. FP is by design a low-pass filter that enforces smoothness, which harms performance when labeled classes or feature patterns vary rapidly across edges. Although the authors mention in the Conclusion that “a more general learnable diffusion could be adopted,” no concrete directions or preliminary experiments are presented. As such, the method appears narrowly tailored to homophilic settings, limiting its real-world applicability to only certain types of networks.

Additional Observations and Suggestions:
- On Novelty: It would strengthen the paper to more explicitly contrast FP’s update matrix Ã (normalized adjacency) with the typical propagation operator in LP (often I – αL or αÃ). If the derivation indeed yields a different normalization or boundary‐conditioning scheme with demonstrable benefits, the authors should highlight these distinctions and, ideally, provide ablation studies showing performance differences caused by those choices.
- Empirical Baselines: I recommend adding the following baselines for completeness, especially at high missing rates (≥90%):
  1. Pure label propagation for node classification (no GNN or feature reconstruction).
  2. DeepWalk or node2vec embeddings (trained on the same graph if features are missing) plus an off-the-shelf classifier.
  3. Perhaps a simple Graph Heat Kernel embedding (Fourier-based low-pass features) as a direct analogue to FP’s spectral smoothing.
  Reporting these results would clarify whether FP+GNN is truly improving over classical topology-only approaches or whether it merely matches them.
- Heterophily and Learnable Diffusion: Since the authors already connect FP to neural PDE formulations, a natural extension would be to parameterize the diffusion operator—e.g., edge-specific conductances or a learned attention mechanism—so that the model can adapt to both homophilic and heterophilic patterns. Even a small pilot experiment on one dataset with moderate homophily (e.g., synthetic graph with homophily ≈0.5) could illustrate the promise of this direction.
- Feature‐Channel Dependence: As noted in the Conclusion, FP treats feature channels independently. In graphs where feature channels are correlated (e.g., categorical features one‐hot encoded across several bits), it might be beneficial to diffuse multi‐dimensional features jointly via a Kronecker‐structured diffusion or a small channel‐mixing MLP at each iteration. An ablation comparing independent vs. joint channel diffusion could be illuminating.

Conclusion and Recommendation:
The idea of using a diffusion-based preprocessing step to impute missing node features is appealing, and the experimental results under extreme missingness are impressive in their robustness and speed. However, the technical contribution is far too close to classical label propagation, and the empirical evaluation omits critical topology-only baselines that could dwarf the purported gains of FP. For these reasons, I am not convinced that the paper meets the bar for a novelty contribution at ICLR. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

title: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a deep neural net-based dereverberation algorithm that uses both audio and video modalities. Based on the observation that a visual scene captured by a camera conveys information that is related to room characteristics, the authors propose a visually informed audio dereverberation method that aims to extract clean, anechoic speech from reverberant speech. In doing so, they first construct a large audio-visual dataset synthesized using a 3D simulator for real-world scanned environments and LibriSpeech data. They then train deep neural networks that take as inputs both visual data (RGB and depth images) and audio data (reverberant speech), and output clean speech. When training, two types of losses - one for clean speech spectrogram estimation and the other for reverb-visual matching - are used. Through the experiments with several downstream tasks for speech, they showed that the proposed audio-visual dererberation method outperforms the baseline models, both for synthetic and real-world test data.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation
- paper is well organized and straightforward to follow
- solid experiments with ablation studies and in-depth analyses
- supplementary materials provide useful information not explained/described in the main text

Weaknesses:
- some of the key arguments are not sufficiently validated
- contributions are marginal and limited
- experiments and evaluation are not thorough

The key idea in this work is that visual input captured by a camera conveys information that characterizes room acoustics. With all the experiments and ablation studies, however, I don't believe that the authors provide sufficient information that support the aforementioned key hypothesis. The authors did take into account this matter in two ways: first, they included a reverb-visual matching loss term to penalize a matching between random speech and visual input, but little experiment or analysis was performed on this loss term - varying the weight or using different loss functions, for example. With or without using a reverb-visual matching loss was included in the ablation studies, but the effect was not significant compared with other methods. The performance was better with the audio-only model even in speech quality metric (PESQ). The second way is to use a random image instead of using the image that matches the room impulse response. In such scenario, the images does not carry any information relevant to the room acoustics, and thus the performance should be worse than or comparable to that of the audio-only method. However, the performance is better in general and is even best for some metric. Further experiments and deeper analysis are warranted to investigate this issue.

Another point that I hope to be added is the comparison of the model efficiency. Adding a visual embedding network increases the number of model parameters and computational costs. The proposed method is likely to work in real-time scenarios, and possibly on small devices, the efficiency should also be considered.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest to include R-vectors approaches as another baseline for comparison.

The idea is interesting and somewhat novel, but the experimental validation is not thorough enough to support the key idea of reverb-visual matching nor to provide sufficient evidences that visual stream does convey information about room acoustics.
