reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper describes an audio de-reverberation method which integrates (panoramic, rgb+depth map) visual input with a spectrogram U-Net to estimate a de-reverberated spectrogram and recover the original clean signal.
The proposed method is compared to several baseline (audio-only) de-reverberation methods on speech enhancement, recognition, and speaker verification problems using both synthetic and real data.
Several ablations of the proposed method are compared, along with some quantitative error analysis investigating the effects of distance and environment.
The proposed method appears to consistently improve on speech recognition and speaker verification, and perform about on par with prior work on speech enhancement (as measured by PESQ).
reviews.main_review:
This paper takes an interesting and novel approach to de-reverberation.
The paper is well written, and each component of the method is well justified.
The qualitative analysis (e.g. figure 4) is a nice touch, and helps shed some light on the behavior of the proposed method (and baselines).
That said, I do have a few questions and suggestions.

My primary criticism is in the reporting of the evaluations (tables 1, 2, 3).
Each setting is summarized by a single average score, and the "best" number is written in bold with no discussion given to the distribution of scores on the test data or the practical meaning of score differences.
This may give readers an inflated sense of performance here: is PESQ=2.38 really any different from PESQ=2.37? 2.33?  (Likewise for WER, EER, etc.)
There is a brief mention of astronomically low p-values (1.56e-60?) which I expect are primarily driven by the size of the test set, and not the size of the effect.
I would strongly encourage the authors to do a more responsible job of reporting their results: include some error bars, or some other indication of the spread of the data!

The proposed method takes an interesting approach to optimizing both magnitude and phase (eqs 1 and 2) for the output spectrogram.
The Griffin-Lim algorithm is then used to refine the phase estimate, with a vague statement of this working better than directly using predicted phase or GL with random initialization.
While I won't ask for more experiments on this, I think the point deserves a bit more explanation than what is given here, especially since there is no ablation study on the phase reconstruction loss.  (Nor is there any discussion of hyper-parameter tuning for equation 4 beyond the settings reported in the appendix.)


I found Figure 5 (qualitative examples) to be pretty difficult to parse, and I say this as a reader with (probably) above-average experience reading spectrograms.
With a bit of squinting I can see what the authors are getting at, but it would be easier to read if the spectrograms were A) larger, and B) plotted on a logarithmic frequency axis so that the low frequencies (where most of the action is) occupy more of the visual real estate.


Finally, it was nice to see the results in table 3, which show quite a strong effect on the performance of the system depending on differences in distance and room geometry.
Not much is said about the geometric differences in the environments though, either for real or simulated data.
In particular, it looks from figure 2 as though there may be configurations of speaker/listener that have no direct path, in which one might say that everything observed is "reverb".
If this is the case, it would be interesting to see how performance (table 1) differs (if at all) in the presence or absence of a direct path.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I enjoyed this paper!  I think some of the reporting could be done better, but this is a minor complaint.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

I found Figure 5 (qualitative examples) to be pretty difficult to parse, and I say this as a reader with (probably) above-average experience reading spectrograms.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this paper, the authors introduce a novel audio-visual dereverberation approach. They propose a Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio (VIDA) model for dereverberation. The authors also create synthetic/simulated datasets and real-world data for experimentation. Finally, they show the impact of the proposed VIDA model on several speech tasks including, recognition, enhancement, and speaker verification. The results are encouraging. The main contribution of this work is the use of visual information as an auxiliary input for dereverberation.
reviews.main_review:
While authors suggest visual information is helpful, it isn't reflected in the results. In Table 1, if we compare the "Audio-only dereverb." and "VIDA" results, the gains are very modest. The PESQ 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT 3.76 to 3.66, and SID EER 2.61 to 2.40. This raises the question about the effectiveness of the proposed approach. I feel the majority of the dereverberation is still learned by the audio model. This should be investigated. 

Overall, the technical contribution is limited to a new dataset and the addition of ResNet for visual information processing. Similar approaches have been used in the audio-visual enhancement with lip information.

Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors introduce a novel audio-visual dereverberation approach. They propose a Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio (VIDA) model for dereverberation. The authors also create synthetic/simulated datasets and real-world data for experimentation. Finally, they show the impact of the proposed VIDA model on several speech tasks including, recognition, enhancement, and speaker verification. The results are encouraging. The main contribution of this work is the use of visual information as an auxiliary input for dereverberation.

Main Review:
While authors suggest visual information is helpful, it isn't reflected in the results. In Table 1, if we compare the "Audio-only dereverb." and "VIDA" results, the gains are very modest. The PESQ 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT 3.76 to 3.66, and SID EER 2.61 to 2.40. This raises the question about the effectiveness of the proposed approach. I feel the majority of the dereverberation is still learned by the audio model. This should be investigated. 

Overall, the technical contribution is limited to a new dataset and the addition of ResNet for visual information processing. Similar approaches have been used in the audio-visual enhancement with lip information.

Summary Of The Review:
I believe the manuscript in its current form offers a very limited contribution to the research community, and the contribution of visual information in dereverberation is minimal.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

title: Layer-wise Adaptive Model Aggregation for Scalable Federated Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a novel adaptive model aggregating schema under a federated learning setting, where it effective reduces the communication overhead without significantly sacrificing the generalization performance. Both empirical study and theoretical analysis are included to justify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
Effectively reducing the communication overhead is an important problem for federated learning, with the recent advance of layer-wise model freezing training, this paper manages to extend this idea for network traffic reduction in federated learning. Concretely, I list the main strengths and minor weakness of this paper as below:

Advantages:

+ To leverage layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is a simple but novel idea. According to the empirical study, this method seems to be effective and efficient. 
+ The paper also includes tight convergence analysis for the proposed method. 

Disadvantages:
+ The communication cost calculated in Section 6 is a little sloppy, in practices, there are plenty of system advances managing to hide the communication overhead within the computation slots, e.g. PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704). In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. Perhaps, a more sophisticated cost model would consider both the computation power of the edge device and network condition during training, then model the cost including the mechanism of overlapping communication and computation to estimate the actual runtime.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The idea of utilizing layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is an interesting idea, the paper includes reasonable empirical study and convergence analysis to illustrate the effectiveness of this proposed approach. 
Although some part of the cost model in the simulation is a little sloppy, I tend to advocate the acceptance of this paper.

reviews.text:
Review for “Layer-Wise Adaptive Model Aggregation for Scalable Federated Learning”  
Recommendation: 8 – Accept, good paper

Summary  
This paper proposes FedLAMA, a novel layer‐wise adaptive aggregation scheme for federated learning that dynamically adjusts the communication frequency per layer based on a run‐time discrepancy metric. The key insight is that different layers of a neural network exhibit varying degrees of model drift across clients, so synchronizing “stable” layers less often can save bandwidth without materially affecting convergence or final accuracy. The authors present a practical metric for prioritizing layers (Equation 2), an algorithm for increasing per‐layer aggregation intervals (Algorithm 2), and a rigorous convergence analysis for smooth, non‐convex objectives under non‐IID data (Theorem 5.3). Empirically, FedLAMA demonstrates up to 60% communication savings on CIFAR‐10 and 70% on non‐IID FEMNIST, while matching FedAvg’s accuracy across ResNet20, WideResNet28‐10, and CNN benchmarks.

Strengths  
1. Novelty and Practicality  
   - Extending layer‐wise freezing concepts (e.g., Brock et al. 2017) to federated aggregation is both original and practically valuable. The decision to lengthen aggregation intervals for layers with low unit discrepancy (dl in Equation 2) directly addresses uneven gradient magnitudes reported by You et al. (2019), yet prior federated methods (FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD) treat all layers equally.  
   - The method is plug‐and‐play: one can combine FedLAMA with existing optimizers, compression, or quantization schemes, as noted in the paper’s Future Work section.  

2. Theoretical Rigor  
   - The authors provide a comprehensive convergence proof (Section 5), deriving a bound in Theorem 5.3 that captures the trade‐off between per‐layer interval increase factor φ and error terms involving variance σ² and heterogeneity κ², β².  
   - The analysis highlights a linear speedup in the number of clients when using a suitably diminishing learning rate (Remark 1), reinforcing FedLAMA’s scalability.  

3. Empirical Evaluation  
   - Experiments cover both IID and non‐IID settings, across CIFAR‐10, CIFAR‐100, and the highly heterogeneous FEMNIST dataset with partial device participation.  
   - The results consistently show that FedLAMA with φ = 2 or 4 maintains baseline accuracy (e.g., ≈88.4% on IID CIFAR‐10) while using only ~62%–42% of the communication cost (Tables 1–3, 6–12).  
   - Visualization of layer‐wise communications (Figures 2–3 in main text; Figures 4–6 in Appendix) clearly pinpoints that most savings stem from less‐frequent synchronization of large, later layers.  

4. Clarity and Reproducibility  
   - The algorithm descriptions (Algorithms 1–2) are crisp, and the run‐time metric for layer discrepancy is computationally lightweight.  
   - The Code of Ethics and Reproducibility Statement detail software versions (TensorFlow 2.4.3, MPI), hardware (NVIDIA V100), and will release code upon acceptance.  

Weaknesses  
• Simplified Communication Cost Model  
  - In Section 6, communication cost is measured as the total transmitted parameter count (Equation 9). However, modern frameworks (e.g., PyTorch-DDP (Yadan et al., 2020)) overlap gradient exchange with backward passes and exploit link-level optimizations. As a result, actual wall‐clock runtime often deviates from raw parameter volume. A more comprehensive cost model—accounting for device compute throughput, network bandwidth, and overlapping communication/computation—would strengthen the practical impact.  
  - For instance, when synchronizing every φτ iterations, latency overhead per round can dominate for smaller layers unless pipelining is leveraged. Incorporating a hybrid model that adds a fixed per‐round latency term plus a bandwidth‐proportional term would offer deeper insight into end‐to‐end training time gains.  

• Parameter Sensitivity Across Architectures  
  - While CIFAR‐ResNet20 and WideResNet28‐10 are representative, it would be useful to see performance on transformer‐style or deeper models (e.g., ResNet50) where layer sizes and gradient norms differ more drastically. This could validate that the proposed priority metric generalizes beyond convolutional nets.  

Minor Comments and Suggestions  
– The discussion in Section 4.2 about choosing φ could be expanded to include guidelines based on target communication budgets or accuracy drop thresholds.  
– It would be valuable to compare FedLAMA against recent adaptive aggregation schemes such as FedDyn or FedAdp, which also adjust communication frequency based on gradient statistics.  
– The authors mention that combining FedLAMA with gradient compression is future work; an ablation applying simple quantization (e.g., 8-bit) to the stabilized layers could show immediate benefits.  

Overall Assessment  
FedLAMA addresses a pertinent bottleneck in federated learning—inefficient bandwidth use due to uniform aggregation. The proposed layer‐wise adaptation is well motivated, theoretically grounded, and empirically validated across multiple datasets and heterogeneity regimes. Despite the simplified communication cost analysis, the core idea and its benefits are clearly demonstrated. I therefore recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods. 

http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf

https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.

Main Review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods. 

http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf

https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes an interesting layer-wise model aggregation method to reduce communication cost while trying to keep the same performance with FedAvg. However, the proposed method’s design, theoretical analysis and experiments cannot support its claim.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This paper introduces FedLAMA, a layer‐wise adaptive model aggregation scheme designed to reduce communication cost in federated learning. The authors observe that different layers of a neural network exhibit varying degrees of model discrepancy across clients, so aggregating all layers at the same frequency (as in FedAvg) is inefficient. FedLAMA computes a run‐time metric for layer‐wise discrepancy (Equation 2) and, every φ·τ iterations, adjusts each layer’s aggregation interval (Algorithm 2) to strike a balance between communication cost reduction and convergence. Theoretical analysis (Theorem 5.3) establishes convergence guarantees under smooth, non‐convex objectives and non‐IID data, and empirical results on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and FEMNIST show up to 60–70% communication savings for IID and non-IID settings with only marginal accuracy loss compared to FedAvg.

Strengths:  
– The paper addresses a clear pain point in federated learning, namely bandwidth inefficiency of periodic full‐model aggregation, and proposes a novel layer‐wise scheduling mechanism.  
– The run‐time metric dl (Equation 2) is a practical, low‐overhead proxy for layer discrepancy compared to heavier similarity measures like CKA or SVCCA (Section 2).  
– The convergence analysis in Section 5 is nontrivial, extending federated optimization proofs to a time-varying, layer‐dependent aggregation schedule and yielding a linear speedup result (Remark 1).  
– Empirical results (Tables 1–5, Figures 2–3) comprehensively demonstrate communication reductions on three benchmarks (ResNet20 on CIFAR-10, WRN28-10 on CIFAR-100, and a CNN on FEMNIST), under both IID and Dirichlet-partitioned non-IID data.  
– The layer‐wise breakdown in Figure 2 clearly shows that large output‐side layers—those with low dl—are aggregated less frequently, explaining where the bulk of communication savings originate.

Weaknesses and Major Concerns:

1) Limited Baselines — The experimental evaluation uses only FedAvg as the baseline aggregation scheme (φ=1 in Tables 1–5). Although FedAvg is the canonical example of periodic full aggregation, other federated learning variants such as FedProx, FedNova, and SCAFFOLD share the same synchronization rhythm but introduce modifications to local objectives, server‐side correction, or normalization. Without direct comparison to these periodic full‐aggregation methods, it is hard to conclude that FedLAMA uniformly outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives.  
   – For example, FedProx (Li et al., 2018) adds a proximal term to stabilize heterogeneity, and SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020) uses variance reduction through control variates; both could interact differently with layer‐wise scheduling.  
   – Prior “layerwise asynchronous” or “temporally weighted” approaches (e.g., Chen et al., 2019) also adjust aggregation timing per layer; discussing or benchmarking against such schemes (including the asynchronous FL approach of Xie et al., 2019) would strengthen the paper’s claims on novelty and practical gains.  
   – A minimal set of additional ablations—such as FedProx with φτ or SCAFFOLD with layer-wise skipping—would contextualize the benefits of FedLAMA beyond the FedAvg baseline.

2) Trade-off Between Accuracy and Communication — While FedLAMA achieves up to 70% communication savings (Table 3) with “marginal” accuracy drop, the absolute accuracy deltas for some settings are nonnegligible (e.g., CIFAR-10 non-IID with φ=4 shows a 2–3% drop in Table 4). The paper reports relative savings (e.g., 42.4% of baseline cost), but does not quantify wall‐clock or end-to-end training time, nor does it frame how much extra training one would need to recover lost accuracy.  
   – In many industrial FL deployments—especially when total training runs are measured in hours or days—a 2% loss in test accuracy may not be acceptable, even if communication is halved. It would help to report absolute communication volume (in GB) and wall‐clock timing on realistic network bandwidths to demonstrate tangible throughput improvements.  
   – A discussion of how to tune φ and τ for a given accuracy budget (Remark 2 in Section 5.2) is present, but concrete guidelines—perhaps a small “knob‐turning” study—would make FedLAMA more practical for practitioners.

3) Clarity of Hyperparameter Sensitivity — Section 6 provides tables for IID vs. non-IID performance and some φ/τ sweeps, but it remains unclear how sensitive FedLAMA is to the choice of base interval τ and increase factor φ across different architectures and datasets.  
   – For instance, in Table 6 (CIFAR-10 IID) φ=2 gives near-zero accuracy change, while φ=4 induces ~2% drop. But how does one pick φ if one only has a limited validation set? A plot of accuracy vs. φ for a few representative workloads (similar to Figure 1’s δℓ vs. λℓ curves) would clarify this trade-off curve.  
   – The impact of partial device participation (active ratio below 100%) on layer‐wise discrepancy is only briefly touched upon in Table 3 but not deeply analyzed. How stable are the dl metrics when only a subset of clients participates? Does sporadic participation amplify or dampen the benefits of layer skipping?

Minor Points & Suggestions:

– In Algorithm 2, sorting individual dl values across L layers costs O(L log L) per synchronization. An explicit note on the negligible overhead relative to gradient computation (as claimed below Figure 3) would preempt concerns about runtime cost.  
– While the paper states in Section 2 that FedLAMA could be “harmonized” with gradient compression or low-rank updates, it would be useful to speculate briefly on whether combining layer skipping with 1-bit quantization (Alistarh et al., 2017) compounds or diminishes gains.  
– A short discussion on privacy implications: since FedLAMA delays synchronization for some layers, does that change vulnerability to gradient inversion or reconstruction attacks for those layers? Even a “no worse than FedAvg” statement would reassure readers.

Overall Recommendation:  
The proposed FedLAMA brings an interesting perspective to adaptive aggregation by leveraging layer‐wise discrepancy metrics and provides solid theoretical justification. However, the empirical comparison is too narrowly focused on FedAvg, and practical trade-offs between accuracy loss and real‐world communication savings need more substantiation. I recommend “Marginally below the acceptance threshold” (Rating: 5).  

Despite its methodological novelty and strong convergence analysis, the current experimental validation should be broadened to include other periodic full‐aggregation schemes and more concrete wall-clock communication metrics before FedLAMA can be deemed a practical solution for federated learning deployments.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors propose a layer-wise model aggregation scheme in federated learning cases to reduce the communication cost. Specifically, they quantified the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusted the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner. By increasing the aggregation intervals and relaxing the aggregation frequency, the method can reduce the communication cost in federated learning cases. The experimental results show it can reduce the communication cost for IID data and non-IID data compared to FedAvg.

Main Review:
They provide a communication-efficient federated optimization and relax the aggregation frequency by quantifying the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusting the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner.

Some questions are the following.

1）The baseline is FedAvg, too few. Why not compare with other the periodic full aggregation scheme (e.g., FedProx, FedNova, SCAFFOLD, etc.), even some synchronized schemes? the synchronized schemes refer to,

[1] Chen, Yang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Yaochu Jin. "Communication-efficient federated deep learning with
layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation." IEEE transactions on neural
networks and learning systems 31.10 (2019): 4229-4238.

[2] Xie, C., Koyejo, S., & Gupta, I. (2019). Asynchronous federated optimization. arXiv preprint
arXiv:1903.03934. 

2）FedLAMA seems to lead to some accuracy drop as the experiments show. They do not show the total time in terms of communication cost, just show the reduced ratio for communication. It is not clear about absolute earnings with sacrificing the accuracy. In some cases, especially in industry, the model's accuracy matters more than training time (e.g., the total training time is within several hours.)

Summary Of The Review:
Thanks to the authors for their efforts for their theoretical analysis and experimentally proof of the proposed federated optimization scheme. The experimental results are a little weak to show the effectiveness and the practicality.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a new idea for reducing communication costs. But the theoretical analysis has some flaws.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

The submitted paper addresses an important challenge in federated learning: reducing communication overhead by adaptively adjusting aggregation frequency on a per-layer basis (FedLAMA). The authors propose a novel layer-wise model discrepancy metric (Equation 2), design an interval adjustment algorithm (Algorithms 1–2), and provide convergence analysis under non‐IID data (Section 5). Empirically, FedLAMA achieves up to 60–70% communication savings over FedAvg on CIFAR‐10/100 and FEMNIST while maintaining comparable accuracy (Section 6).

Strengths  
1. Relevance and Novelty  
   * The work targets a key bottleneck in federated learning—inefficient full‐model synchronization—and breaks from the convention of uniform aggregation by prioritizing layers based on their run‐time discrepancy (Section 4).  
   * The layer‐wise adaptive aggregation concept extends beyond existing adaptive‐interval or compression methods. By dynamically lengthening intervals for low‐discrepancy layers, FedLAMA offers a fresh angle on communication savings.  

2. Theoretical Contribution  
   * The paper presents a convergence framework (Lemma 5.1) and bounds on model discrepancy (Lemma 5.2), culminating in Theorem 5.3, which quantifies the trade‐off among learning rate η, aggregation intervals τₗ, the maximum interval τₘₐₓ, and heterogeneity parameters β², κ².  
   * The analysis explicitly handles smooth, non‐convex objectives and non‐IID data, extending prior FedAvg‐style proofs to the layer‐wise asynchronous setting (Appendix A.1).  

3. Experimental Validation  
   * Experiments span three representative image‐classification benchmarks—CIFAR‐10, CIFAR‐100, and FEMNIST—with both IID and Dirichlet‐based non‐IID splits, and partial device participation (Section 6).  
   * Training curves (Figures 2–3, 6) clearly demonstrate that FedLAMA maintains convergence speed while reducing communication volume, and ablations over φ and τ empirically support the theoretical insights.  

Weaknesses  
1. Errors in Theoretical Derivations  
   * In Section 5.1, Equation (15) (which is used to decompose the difference in full‐batch gradients) does not correctly apply the equality 2⟨a,b⟩=‖a‖²+‖b‖²−‖a−b‖². As a result, the subsequent bound on T₁ in Equation (24) is flawed.  
   * Similarly, Equation (25), which attempts to bound the variance term T₂ by splitting stochastic gradients into unbiased noise plus full gradients, omits cross terms and understates dependence across clients. These omissions propagate into Lemma 5.1’s final inequality.  
   * Without correcting these two steps, the stated convergence upper bound in Theorem 5.3 lacks a firm foundation. A more careful decomposition, explicitly accounting for E[⟨gradient errors, gradient estimates⟩], is needed.  

2. Missing Learning‐Rate Constraint in Lemma 5.2  
   * Lemma 5.2 establishes a bound on the average model discrepancy over K rounds, but the result holds only if η satisfies 2(τₘₐₓ−1)L·η²<1 (see the definition of A=2η²τₘₐₓ(τₘₐₓ−1)L² in Appendix A.1.2). However, this constraint is not stated alongside Lemma 5.2 in the main text. Without it, readers cannot verify whether the chosen η in experiments satisfies the regime required for that bound.  

3. Loose Convergence Rate Compared to Prior Work  
   * Remark 1 claims linear speedup when K=O(m³), but recent results for distributed non‐convex optimization achieve linear speedup with K=O(m) under momentum or variance‐reduction [1]. The dependency on m³ here is substantially worse and should be explicitly discussed.  
   * The paper would benefit from a comparison to the bound in [1] (“On the Linear Speedup Analysis of Communication‐Efficient Momentum SGD for Distributed Non‐Convex Optimization”). Even if FedLAMA’s layer‐wise adaptation introduces unavoidable looseness, this gap should be highlighted and explained.  

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
• Section 5, Equation (15) and (25): please revisit the derivations of T₁ and T₂ in Lemma 5.1, ensuring the cross terms from gradient variance and heterogeneity are properly bounded. An alternative approach is to apply the standard smoothness inequality directly to F(uₖ₊₁) and then isolate ‖uₖ−uᵢₖ‖² terms without erroneous expansions.  
• In the statement of Lemma 5.2 (main text, pg. 6), add the explicit requirement η<1/√[2(τₘₐₓ−1)]·1/L so that practitioners can choose η to satisfy the condition.  
• In the discussion of Remark 1 (pg. 6), clearly contrast FedLAMA’s scaling law (K=O(m³)) with existing FedAvg variants. If possible, tighten the analysis by leveraging additional structure from layer‐wise aggregation to reduce the dependence on m.  
• Appendix A could include a small worked example or numerical check of the bounds on CIFAR‐10 to illustrate that the theoretical constraints on η and τₘₐₓ align with the chosen hyperparameters.  

Minor Points  
– In Algorithm 2, it would aid reproducibility to specify how ties in sorting dₗ are broken.  
– The notation dl (Equation 2) could be renamed to Δₗ to avoid confusion with dimension dim(·).  
– In Figures 2–3, annotating the “cross point” of δₗ and 1−λₗ with vertical lines would make the explanation in Section 4 more immediate.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
FedLAMA introduces a compelling layer‐wise adaptive aggregation idea that is empirically effective in reducing communication cost. The breadth of experiments and the proposed run‐time discrepancy metric are valuable contributions. However, the core theoretical analysis contains non‐trivial errors (Equations 15, 25) and omits critical η constraints in Lemma 5.2. Moreover, the convergence bound K=O(m³) is significantly looser than in related distributed SGD analyses. These issues weaken the paper’s main claims on convergence guarantees. At this stage, I recommend **rating 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**. With careful revision of the proofs and clarification of learning‐rate constraints, this work could make a solid contribution to the federated learning literature.

title: Stability based Generalization Bounds for Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the generalization of iterative noisy learning algorithms. First of all, the authors provide a new generalization bound based on the Hellinger distance. This bound is basically based on the Hellinger distance between the output of the algorithm when one of the points in the training set is replaced with a fresh sample. They they use this bound to provide a generalization error guarantee for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithm which is defined as W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t conditioned on the past iterated follows an exponential family distribution which for instance include SGLD. For this broad class, they provide a unified generalization bound that includes SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. Finally, the authors provide numerical study of their bound for several benchmarks and show that their bound achieves better estimate of generalization error.
reviews.main_review:
- Using the generalization bound in Theorem: In proposition 1, the authors provide a generalization bound based on the distributional stability in terms of Hellinger distance. However, in most of the results in the paper they mainly use the upper bound based on KL divergence (Prop. 2). Is that correct?  

- The dependence of the learning rate on the optimization trajectory: In your bound alpha_t depends on the dataset and trajectory. It basically depends on some sort of Lipschitz constant of the loss function. For instance in SGLD, alpha_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperatures. Therefore, I think it can limit us to use very small learning rates. Also, how did you make sure that this constraint is satisfied in your numerical studies?

- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.: Your bound is very similar to the generalization bound in Haghifam et al'2020. As you mentioned the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is stated for LD algorithm. Recently, the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is extended to SGLD in [1]. The bound in Rodríguez-Gálvez et al has worse dependence on n (1/sqrt(n)), however, they have a decaying factor in their bound to compensate using of the chain rule. It would be nice to numerically compare your bound with the bound in [1]. 

- Applying your framework for analyzing SGD:  What are the roadblocks for applying your bound to analyzing SGD?

- Surrogate loss: I think you should distinguish the loss function that we use to measure the generalization error with the loss function that is used for training. You should use different notations. 


[1] "On random subset generalization error bounds and the stochastic gradient langevin dynamics algorithm", Borja Rodríguez-Gálvez, Germán Bassi, Ragnar Thobaben, Mikael Skoglund

This paper studies the generalization of iterative noisy learning algorithms. First of all, the authors provide a new generalization bound based on the Hellinger distance. This bound is basically based on the Hellinger distance between the output of the algorithm when one of the points in the training set is replaced with a fresh sample. They they use this bound to provide a generalization error guarantee for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithm which is defined as W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t conditioned on the past iterated follows an exponential family distribution which for instance include SGLD. For this broad class, they provide a unified generalization bound that includes SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. Finally, the authors provide numerical study of their bound for several benchmarks and show that their bound achieves better estimate of generalization error.

Main Review:
- Using the generalization bound in Theorem: In proposition 1, the authors provide a generalization bound based on the distributional stability in terms of Hellinger distance. However, in most of the results in the paper they mainly use the upper bound based on KL divergence (Prop. 2). Is that correct?  

- The dependence of the learning rate on the optimization trajectory: In your bound alpha_t depends on the dataset and trajectory. It basically depends on some sort of Lipschitz constant of the loss function. For instance in SGLD, alpha_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperatures. Therefore, I think it can limit us to use very small learning rates. Also, how did you make sure that this constraint is satisfied in your numerical studies?

- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.: Your bound is very similar to the generalization bound in Haghifam et al'2020. As you mentioned the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is stated for LD algorithm. Recently, the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is extended to SGLD in [1]. The bound in Rodríguez-Gálvez et al has worse dependence on n (1/sqrt(n)), however, they have a decaying factor in their bound to compensate using of the chain rule. It would be nice to numerically compare your bound with the bound in [1]. 

- Applying your framework for analyzing SGD:  What are the roadblocks for applying your bound to analyzing SGD?

- Surrogate loss: I think you should distinguish the loss function that we use to measure the generalization error with the loss function that is used for training. You should use different notations.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper improves generalization bounds based on the notion of stability. The notion of stability measures the effect of resampling a data point to the output of the algorithm. The paper improves known analyses in two directions: 
- It proposes to use the notion of expected stability rather than uniform stability, which should to be tighter.
- It generalizes the analysis to noises in an exponential family rather than Gaussian noise.
reviews.main_review:
This paper provides several technical improvements to a recent line of work that I am not familiar with. Thus I can hardly review that relevance  and novelty of these improvements (and I am thus putting a low confidence score). I am concerned by the fact that the article is hard to read in a self-contained way. The authors largely comment the articulation of their work with similar works, but almost never comment the relevance of their work for a wider community. 

Another concern is that the article provides two improvements listed above, but I could not perceive why it was coherent to present them in a common article. This might add some confusion to the paper. 

As a naive question, I would like to ask why expected stability bounds are preferred. It seems that they involve expectations that are hard to compute analytically, at best one can approximate these expectations by sampling. How should these bounds be used in the end? In practice, to choose stopping criteria for algorithms, for instance? Or in theory, to analyze the performance of some algorithms?

I found the generalization from Gaussian noise to exponential families novel and interesting.

In Section 2, I find difficult to determine what are your contributions and what was already known. For instance, Proposition 2 seems well-known right? Why not giving some references rather than proving it? Similarly, to what extent was Proposition 1 known?

Minor comments:
-  Section 2: "D be an unknown distribution OVER Z" (not "of")
- Why do you introduce the notation W_{0:(t-1)} = w_{0:(t-1)}? I am under the impression that this is twice the same object, but I might misunderstand.
- You sometimes use the notation KL(P,Q) and KL(P||Q).
- Conflict of notation between S_0 and S_n (for n=0!).
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I am not confident with my review as this paper is quite technical and I am not familiar with this line of work. I would appreciate having more insights about the application of the new techniques and the relevance of exposing them together in this paper.

reviews.text:
Review for “Stability Based Generalization Bounds for Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics”

Decision: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary  
This paper aims to advance stability‐based generalization bounds for noisy iterative learning algorithms. Specifically, it (i) replaces uniform stability with an expected stability notion—argued to yield tighter, distribution‐dependent bounds—and (ii) extends the analysis of Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (SGLD) from Gaussian noise to arbitrary exponential family noise, thereby unifying and generalizing existing bounds. The authors introduce Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics (EFLD) as a framework capturing both SGLD (Gaussian case) and noisy Sign‐SGD (Bernoulli case), derive data‐dependent generalization bounds with O(1/n) sample dependence and gradient‐incoherence terms, and provide empirical evidence that these bounds are non‐vacuous and sharper than prior work.

Strengths  
- The move from uniform to expected stability is compelling, since it replaces worst‐case suprema with expectations, potentially yielding tighter bounds.  
- Generalizing from Gaussian to general exponential family noise (EFLD) is novel and broadens applicability to algorithms like noisy Sign‐SGD and quantized SGD.  
- The paper provides both theoretical results (new generalization and optimization guarantees) and experiments on MNIST, Fashion‐MNIST, CIFAR‐10/100 that show non‐vacuous bounds and improved tightness over prior bounds.  

Main Review  
While the paper presents technically interesting ideas, I encountered several concerns that prevent me from fully endorsing it at this time.

1. Self‐Containment and Readability  
   The manuscript is dense and presumes substantial familiarity with multiple threads of prior work (Mou et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020; Negrea et al., 2019; Haghifam et al., 2020). As a result, it is hard to read in a self‐contained fashion. For example, when the authors state in Section 2 that “expected stability can be bounded by the expected Le Cam Style Divergence,” a reader unfamiliar with Le Cam divergences must refer externally to Sason & Verdú (2016) or dig through related appendices. It would help to provide a brief intuitive sketch or toy example of expected vs. uniform stability in the main text. Similarly, the summary of prior results on SGLD in the Introduction references multiple bounds with different O(1/n) or O(1/√n) dependencies, but a tabular comparison or schematic figure summarizing these trade‐offs would greatly enhance clarity.  

2. Coherence of the Two Main Contributions  
   The paper advances two distinct ideas—expected stability and exponential‐family noise—yet it is not entirely clear why these belong in a single manuscript. The connection between expected stability and EFLD is presented formally (through Lemma 1 and Theorem 2), but the narrative could better explain why expected stability is especially natural or advantageous when one moves beyond Gaussian noise. At present, the reader may wonder whether the two improvements are orthogonal and could be split into separate studies, possibly reducing confusion. A clearer roadmap in the Introduction, explicitly describing how these two threads interlock, would help justify the unification.

3. Practical Use of Expected Stability Bounds  
   As a “naïve question,” why are expected stability bounds preferred in practice? The bounds involve expectations over both the data distribution and algorithmic randomness, which in general are hard to compute analytically. In Section 4, the authors approximate these expectations empirically, but more discussion is needed on the computational cost and statistical error of this approximation. For instance, how many Monte Carlo trials of mini‐batch trajectories are required to estimate the bound reliably? Do these bounds suggest practical stopping criteria, hyperparameter tuning guidelines, or algorithm choices? A dedicated paragraph in the Experiments section (or a short discussion section) illustrating how a practitioner would leverage expected stability to make algorithmic decisions would strengthen the work’s relevance.

4. Novelty Versus Prior Results in Section 2  
   In Section 2, Proposition 2 states the well‐known bound between Hellinger and KL divergences (2 H² ≤ KL). This inequality appears in many textbooks (e.g. Pollard, 2002; Sason & Verdú, 2016). Rather than reproving it, I recommend citing a standard reference and moving the proof to an appendix. Similarly, Proposition 1—while nicely stated for expected stability—parallels results in Li et al. (2020) and Mou et al. (2018). The authors should clarify precisely which parts of Proposition 1 are new versus folklore in stability analyses. This would help the reader distinguish the paper’s original contributions from background lemmas.

5. Minor Technical and Notational Issues  
   – In the second paragraph of Section 2, write “D be an unknown distribution over Z” (rather than “of Z”).  
   – The notation W_{0:(t−1)} = w_{0:(t−1)} appears to conflate a random vector of iterates and its realization; please clarify or consolidate.  
   – The paper sometimes writes KL(P, Q) and other times KL(P‖Q). For consistency, please adopt a single notation.  
   – There is a conflict between S₀ defined in Section 2.2 (the set {z₁,…,z_{n−1}}) and any potential use of S₀ for a zero‐length dataset. Consider swapping that symbol or explicitly noting the overload.

Additional Suggestions  
– A high‐level section or figure contrasting uniform, expected, and information‐theoretic stability bounds by their assumptions, dependence on step‐size, Lipschitz constants, and gradient incoherence would help situate the contributions.  
– In the experimental section, it would be instructive to show how the bound evolves during training on a single run, alongside the training and test errors, to demonstrate early‐stopping suggestions.  
– Since EFLD encompasses a wide class of exponential families, a short table listing common special cases (Gaussian, Bernoulli, Poisson, Laplace) and their natural‐parameter interpretations would aid readability.  

Summary  
This paper makes solid technical strides by combining expected stability and exponential‐family noise in a single stability‐based generalization‐bound framework. However, the manuscript would benefit from improved exposition, clearer separation of new versus established results, and more discussion of practical implications. At present, I find the paper is technically sound but not sufficiently accessible or clearly motivated for the broader conference audience.

Rating  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Main Review:
This paper provides several technical improvements to a recent line of work that I am not familiar with. Thus I can hardly review that relevance  and novelty of these improvements (and I am thus putting a low confidence score). I am concerned by the fact that the article is hard to read in a self-contained way. The authors largely comment the articulation of their work with similar works, but almost never comment the relevance of their work for a wider community. 

Another concern is that the article provides two improvements listed above, but I could not perceive why it was coherent to present them in a common article. This might add some confusion to the paper. 

As a naive question, I would like to ask why expected stability bounds are preferred. It seems that they involve expectations that are hard to compute analytically, at best one can approximate these expectations by sampling. How should these bounds be used in the end? In practice, to choose stopping criteria for algorithms, for instance? Or in theory, to analyze the performance of some algorithms?

I found the generalization from Gaussian noise to exponential families novel and interesting.

In Section 2, I find difficult to determine what are your contributions and what was already known. For instance, Proposition 2 seems well-known right? Why not giving some references rather than proving it? Similarly, to what extent was Proposition 1 known?

Minor comments:
-  Section 2: "D be an unknown distribution OVER Z" (not "of")
- Why do you introduce the notation W_{0:(t-1)} = w_{0:(t-1)}? I am under the impression that this is twice the same object, but I might misunderstand.
- You sometimes use the notation KL(P,Q) and KL(P||Q).
- Conflict of notation between S_0 and S_n (for n=0!).

Summary Of The Review:
I am not confident with my review as this paper is quite technical and I am not familiar with this line of work. I would appreciate having more insights about the application of the new techniques and the relevance of exposing them together in this paper.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides generalization guarantees for exponential family sgld which is a more general setup than standard gaussian noise langevin dynamics as it allows for noise settings from any member of the exponential family. The authors point out that the setup they consider is a strict generalization of the standard langevin dynamics, and give another explicit example of sign sgd based generalization. 

The authors use and show gradient discrepancy as opposed to gradient norms as used by Li et al, and gradient incoherence as used by Negrea et al. but the latter have 1/\sqrt{n}. Overall, from useful empirical observations it seems that the bounds proposed by the authors are tighter than these other bounds.
reviews.main_review:
There have been several works that generalize from gaussian settings to the more general exponential family, including the ones that are cited by the authors but also for example monotone retargetting and exponential family PCA. As such there are several tools for such an analysis that are pretty well known now. Other than that, the analysis follows closely with that of Li et al, with few minor but important differences. 

I am not sure if the extension to exponential family is a major contribution. It allows for analysis of signSGD. But given several other works of similar nature, I am not sure if the analysis and the major contribution as claimed, including in the title of the paper justifies a publication. 

It seems, however, that proposing gradient discrepancy itself is a useful contribution. Can the authors highlight the difference in the analysis from Li et al explicitly that led to this difference in the bounds ? Why is this sidelined as a side-effect? Does this follow from the new exponential family based analysis proposed here (it does not seem to be so) ? The experiments are also conducted based on this contribution. As such the main message/contribution of the paper and the empirical section seems disconnected. I am happy to be corrected.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The main contribution of exponential family dynamics seems not very significant. Gradient discrepancy is sidelined as a minor contribution but is focussed on a lot more in the empirical section, which makes the paper seems disconnected.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.

Main Review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Stability Based Generalization Bounds for Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics”

Summary  
This paper introduces Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics (EFLD), a generalization of stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) that injects noise drawn from an arbitrary exponential family rather than just Gaussian. The authors develop a unifying framework based on expected stability and the Le Cam Style Divergence (LSD) to prove data-dependent generalization bounds for these noisy stochastic iterative (NSI) algorithms. They show an O(1/n) dependence on the sample size n and replace the usual gradient norm terms with the (often much smaller) gradient discrepancy or incoherence. As special cases, they recover SGLD and a noisy sign-SGD variant (with Bernoulli noise), including accompanying optimization guarantees. Finally, they report experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR benchmarks to demonstrate that their bounds are non-vacuous and tighter than existing bounds from Li et al. (2020), Negrea et al. (2019), and Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. (2021).

Strengths  
1. General O(1/n) Bound for NSI Algorithms  
   • The paper extends stability-based analyses (Mou et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020) beyond Gaussian noise and gradient descent to cover arbitrary exponential family noise. By bounding expected stability via the expected Le Cam Style Divergence, the authors recover the desirable O(1/n) sample dependence for a very broad class of noisy iterative methods.  
   • This result subsumes prior uniform-stability bounds that required very small step sizes (ηt ≤ σt/K) or had worse 1/√n scaling. The authors clearly highlight in Section 2.2 how their Proposition 1 and Lemma 1 yield simpler proofs and more general applicability than those in Li et al. (2020).  

2. Novel Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics (EFLD)  
   • By introducing EFLD in Section 3, the paper offers a unified view of SGLD, noisy sign-SGD, and even quantized SGD as instances of exponential family noise injections. The detailed Example 3.1 (Gaussian → SGLD) and Example 3.2 (Bernoulli → noisy sign-SGD) showcase this versatility.  
   • Theorem 2 gives a compact bound that depends on the per-step gradient discrepancy ∥∇ℓ(w, zn)−∇ℓ(w, z′)∥² rather than the global Lipschitz constant or raw gradient norms, resulting in much sharper numerical values.  

3. Empirical Validation of Bound Tightness  
   • The experiments in Section 4 on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10/100 systematically compare the new bound against those of Li et al. (2020), Negrea et al. (2019), and Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. (2021). Figures 1(e)–(h) demonstrate that, for identical training trajectories, the proposed bound is orders of magnitude smaller.  
   • The label-corruption study in Figure 2 further illustrates that as gradient discrepancy grows (with more random labels), the bound correctly reflects the worsening generalization, thereby confirming its data-dependence.  

Weaknesses  
1. Lack of State-of-the-Art Empirical Performance  
   • While the theoretical contributions are solid, the paper’s new algorithms (SGLD with exponential-family noise or noisy sign-SGD) do not outperform strong baselines in predictive accuracy. The CNN and ResNet-18 architectures used in Section 4 yield test errors comparable to or worse than those from standard SGD or specialized variants like replica-exchange Langevin dynamics.  
   • The authors acknowledge in Section 4.1 that they did not achieve state-of-the-art results, attributing it to simple architectures and limited hyperparameter tuning. However, without an empirical advantage, it is unclear why one would adopt the more complex EFLD framework in practice.  

2. Omission of Recent Advanced Langevin Variants  
   • The related work discussion (Appendix A) surveys uniform stability and information-theoretic bounds but omits recent successes in replica-exchange Langevin dynamics (e.g., Bernton et al., 2021) and second-order Hamiltonian Monte Carlo methods that have demonstrated strong empirical performance.  
   • It remains an open question whether EFLD could integrate such advanced sampling strategies or achieve similar gains if paired with momentum, temperature schedules, or higher-order discretizations discussed in the literature.  

Overall Recommendation  
This paper offers a well-motivated and technically sound generalization of stability-based bounds for a broad class of noisy stochastic algorithms. The use of expected stability and LSD is both elegant and practically sharper than existing approaches. The introduction of EFLD unifies key algorithms under one framework. However, the lack of compelling empirical gains and omission of more recent Langevin-based optimizers limit the practical impact. I therefore recommend a marginal rejection (Rating 5), noting that acceptance would require stronger experimental evidence or integration with the latest sampling advances.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.

Main Review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Fluency for Novel Object Captioning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a framework that combines masked language models (BERT) and image-text embedding models (CLIP) for novel object captioning. The proposed model significantly improves the fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of the generated images. Extensive experiments are conducted and the comparison between the proposed model and existing models shows that the proposed model performs relatively well.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Weaknesses:

W1: The proposed model combines some existing models, like BERT, CLIP, so one of my concerns is the novelty of the proposed framwork. Plus, the reward to train the model is not novel, which is used in Rotian's work [1] and distilling from BERT for text generation was proposed by Chen [2]. Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposed a strong baseline of novel object captioning via combining BERT and CLIP models, however, the novelty is limited.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: Not applicable
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Main Review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a strong baseline of novel object captioning via combining BERT and CLIP models, however, the novelty is limited.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.
reviews.main_review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there?
